summary: Section 8.15 mentions WGS84 once in a subordinate clause when describing the curve-fit scripts, and mentions disc_radius=20,015 km once without flagging it as πR_Earth. Both are globe fingerprints hiding in plain sight inside the section's own evidence. The stronger version of 'the curve fit IS the model' is 'the curve fit targets — and encodes — the globe coordinate frame, line by line, in comments the author wrote himself.'

fingerprint_1.name: find_curve.py encodes the azimuthal equidistant projection as its fit target via the literal formula r = (90 - lat) * 111.1 km — a globe great-circle arc-length-per-degree identity
fingerprint_1.kernel_of_truth: Section 8.15 correctly identifies test_curve_stretching.py and find_curve.py as the heart of the modelling methodology and correctly notes they 'perform iterative least-squares fitting to WGS84 coordinates.' That is right. What the section does not do is show the READER what the fit target actually is. Without that, a dome defender can say 'WGS84 is just a convenient coordinate frame; our model reinterprets it.' The literal contents of find_curve.py close that door.
fingerprint_1.but_here_is_why_it_fails: find_curve.py line 34 contains the author's own comment: 'Required H(lat) to maintain perfect WGS84 map radius (r = (90-lat)*111.1):' followed by a loop that computes r = (90 - lat) * 111.1 for latitudes 80 through -10 in 10-degree steps. This formula is not a flat-disc coordinate system — it is the azimuthal equidistant projection from the pole on a globe of radius R = 6,371 km. The constant 111.1 km/deg equals π R_Earth / 180 = π × 6,371 / 180 = 111.195 km/deg — the globe's great-circle arc per degree of latitude, known to every cartographer. The dome's 'curve-fitting' script does not fit to raw observations; it fits H(lat) to maintain agreement with the AEP of a sphere. The repository does not just use globe-derived inputs — the repository encodes the globe map formula as the curve-fit target, in code the author wrote and commented himself. The model is an AEP of the globe with an H(lat) fudge layer bolted on.

globe_value.name: Great-circle arc length per degree of latitude
globe_value.derivation: π × R_Earth / 180 = π × 6,371 km / 180 = 111.195 km/deg — the length of one degree of meridian arc on the globe
globe_value.source: Standard geodesy. Equivalent forms: 2π R / 360; ≈ 60 nautical miles/deg. 111.1 is the rounded value used in AEP and flight-planning tools worldwide.
globe_value.AEP_identity: On an azimuthal equidistant projection from the north pole, a point at latitude lat maps to radial distance r = (90 - lat) × 111.1 km from the pole center

dome_value.name: Fit-target radial distance in find_curve.py
dome_value.value: r = (90 - lat) * 111.1 km (literal expression in code)
dome_value.code_location: find_curve.py line 34 (comment) and line 36 (expression in loop body)
dome_value.usage: Computes the 'required' firmament height H_req = r × tan(lat) at each latitude so that H/r matches the globe AEP radius. The script exists to determine what H(lat) profile must be imposed to keep the disc map agreeing with WGS84 at each latitude.

quantitative_link.interpretation: The script is explicitly parameterized on the globe AEP identity. At lat=0, r = 90 × 111.1 = 9,999 km; at lat=-90 (south pole), r = 180 × 111.1 = 19,998 km, matching the dome's disc_radius = 20,015 km to 0.09% — i.e., the disc edge IS the south pole of the globe unrolled by the AEP formula. The entire dome coordinate system collapses to one globe identity in this one file.

verbatim_excerpts: print("Required H(lat) to maintain perfect WGS84 map radius (r = (90-lat)*111.1):")
code_evidence.verification_method: GitHub API read of john09289/predictions/find_curve.py on 2026-04-15. Constants and comments confirmed in-repo.

fingerprint_1.why_it_matters: Section 8.15's deepest argument is 'the curve fit is the model.' That is right but under-specified. The stronger claim is that the curve-fit target is the globe's AEP. This converts the section's position from 'the code is opaque physics-wise' to 'the code is self-documenting: the author wrote a comment telling the reader that the goal is to maintain WGS84 map agreement at each latitude.' The section doesn't need to argue the point — it just needs to quote the comment. This also closes off the 'we use WGS84 as a convenient coordinate frame but our model is independent' defense: the script is not re-expressing observations in disc coordinates, it is demanding that disc coordinates reproduce the globe AEP to within numerical precision.

defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim: The 111.1 km/deg is just a convenient way to convert latitude to distance for humans reading the script. The model itself doesn't depend on 111.1 being π R_Earth/180 — it could use any distance unit.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter: If it were unit-neutral, the script would use a symbolic constant like KM_PER_DEG defined in terms of the dome's own geometry. Instead it uses the rounded globe value 111.1 inline. More importantly, the COMMENT explicitly names the target 'perfect WGS84 map radius' — the fit target is not any pair of distances, it is the specific AEP identity of the globe. The dome defender cannot wave off the comment the author wrote himself.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim_2: The script is just one of many in the repository; it's not clear it represents the model's core methodology.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter_2: Section 8.15 itself singles out find_curve.py and test_curve_stretching.py as the files that generate the 'curve fit' that IS the model. The comment in find_curve.py is therefore a primary source, not a marginal one. If these scripts are not the model, the section's core claim needs separate support. If they are, this comment is the tightest possible confirmation of our argument.

fingerprint_2.name: The three hardcoded 'core parameters' in inject_ai_layer.py are a globe-derived triplet, not an independent disc-native set — the section cites them flat without flagging the identities
fingerprint_2.kernel_of_truth: The existing section 8.15 paragraph states: 'inject_ai_layer.py hardcodes the dome's core parameters (disc_radius = 20,015 km, firmament_height = 9,086 km, sun_altitude = 5,733 km) as static values for AI consumption — not as outputs of any calculation.' That observation is factually correct — lines 89-93 of inject_ai_layer.py (verified 2026-04-15) do contain these literals.
fingerprint_2.but_here_is_why_it_fails: Calling them 'hardcoded with no derivation' is a weaker argument than calling them what they actually are — namely, globe quantities in disguise. The identity disc_radius = 20,015 km ≈ π × R_Earth is already documented elsewhere in our review (see SEC-2.9, SEC-5.14 fingerprint 2, SEC-6.6). Section 8.15 is a natural place to complete the triplet: all three 'core parameters' are tied to globe geometry. disc_radius = 20,015 km sets the disc edge at the AEP antipode (π × 6,371 = 20,011.7 km; match 0.02%). firmament_height = 9,086 km sits between the Schumann quarter-wave c/(4 × 7.83 Hz) = 9,572 km (the value the section itself quotes) and the distance-fit supremum 8,537 km documented in SEC-2.1 — i.e., it is a compromise between two globe-derived constraints the author is balancing. sun_altitude = 5,733 km is chosen so that at the Tropic of Cancer disc-radius r ≈ (90 - 23.44) × 111.1 ≈ 7,390 km, the solar elevation arctan(5,733 / 7,390) ≈ 37.8° reproduces approximately the observed noon-sun elevation at that latitude on the equinox — another globe-boundary-condition calibration. The section's current framing treats these three values as arbitrary constants; the stronger framing is that they are the AEP radius, a Schumann-adjacent height, and a solar-elevation-calibrated height — no one of which is free once you fix the others.

globe_values.firmament_height_vs_Schumann_quarterwave: 9,086 km vs c/(4 × 7.83 Hz) = 9,572 km — 5.4% off; the section itself quotes this formula
globe_values.sun_altitude_vs_tropic_elevation: 5,733 km implies arctan(5733 / 7390) = 37.8° at Tropic of Cancer AEP radius — close to the observed noon-sun elevation at equinox at that latitude (not a random pick)

dome_values.verification_method: GitHub API read of john09289/predictions/inject_ai_layer.py on 2026-04-15

fingerprint_2.why_it_matters: Strengthens the section's 'no forward model' argument by showing that even the constants being hardcoded are not arbitrary. 'No derivation' is a weaker claim than 'no derivation needed because the values are already globe quantities relabeled.' This complements SEC-5.14's fingerprint 2 (recalc_v51.py Redge = 20015) by extending the same argument to inject_ai_layer.py, which is the section 8.15 specifically singles out.

defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim: A 5.4% discrepancy between firmament_height and the Schumann quarter-wavelength isn't a fingerprint — that's just a different number.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter: The match doesn't need to be exact to be a fingerprint — the question is whether the value sits on or near a globe-derived locus. 9,086 km is close enough to the Schumann quarter-wave that the section itself uses the Schumann formula to contextualize it. A genuinely free disc parameter would not be within 5% of a specific globe-cavity constant; the more natural explanation is that the author is balancing Schumann-compatibility against distance-fit residuals.

suggested_patch.target: part8 section 8.15, the existing paragraph about test_curve_stretching.py and find_curve.py
suggested_patch.current_text: test_curve_stretching.py and find_curve.py perform iterative least-squares fitting to WGS84 coordinates. There is no deeper physical derivation underneath — the curve fit is the model.
suggested_patch.proposed_replacement: test_curve_stretching.py and find_curve.py perform iterative least-squares fitting to WGS84 coordinates. The fit target is the globe's own coordinate system: find_curve.py line 34 contains the author's comment "Required H(lat) to maintain perfect WGS84 map radius (r = (90-lat)*111.1)", followed by a loop that computes r = (90 - lat) × 111.1 km at each latitude. That formula is the azimuthal equidistant projection from the north pole on a sphere of radius R_Earth — the constant 111.1 km/deg equals π R_Earth / 180 = 111.195 km/deg to four parts in 10,000. The 'curve fit' is not just calibrated against globe data; it is structurally designed to reproduce the globe AEP at each latitude. There is no deeper physical derivation underneath — the curve fit is the model, and the model is the globe's AEP with an H(lat) fudge layer added to match distances that pure AEP gets wrong.
suggested_patch.tldr_patch: No change needed — the existing TLDR ('no forward model exists...') remains accurate; this patch only strengthens the supporting paragraph.

newsworthy: Dome's own curve-fitting script carries an author comment explicitly targeting 'perfect WGS84 map radius (r = (90-lat)*111.1)' — the globe AEP identity, line-coded as the fit target
notes_and_caveats: Fingerprint 1 is high-confidence because the evidence is the author's own comment in his own code. The only interpretive choice is whether the comment describes a primary methodology or a throwaway script. Section 8.15 already treats find_curve.py as primary, so this is consistent.

Fingerprint 2 is moderate-confidence because the sun_altitude and firmament_height links to globe quantities are not as clean as the disc_radius = π R identity. Recommend presenting the triplet as a pattern rather than claiming each number matches a single globe constant to 0.01%.
This finding does NOT contradict or modify the SEC-5.14 fingerprint 2 (recalc_v51.py Redge = 20015). It extends the pattern to a different file that SEC-5.15 explicitly cites.

Consider-the-other-side: a fair defender might say the author uses 111.1 as a 'didactic' constant to illustrate the argument, not as the actual fit target. Counter: the comment literally says 'to MAINTAIN perfect WGS84 map radius' — the script's purpose is to enforce AEP agreement, not illustrate it.
No defense neutralization performed — no Cycle 3+ curmudgeon review exists for SEC-5.15 (only one cycle-1 review, which we read and used to inform this analysis).
